issue_title: Huge image size for a simple PHP App
issue_body:
Hi, 
I'm deploying a simple PHP App (it's the php-getting-started example of Heroku), and it runs perfectly. My doubt is when I check the image size, 1.516GB , wow it's really huge for this simple App. 
I deployed another PHP applications and I get exactly the same size.
I saw that the Application is builded from deis/slugrunner that has a size of 833MB (https://imagelayers.io/?images=deis%2Fslugrunner:latest).

I want to deploy a lot of apps, I will have at least this image size for all my apps? Could I reduce it?

I have a deis cluster (one node) on a bare metal instance sitting behind a corporate proxy.
It was a little challenging but pushing a project with a Dockerfile is working.
However following after https://github.com/deis/deis/issues/1421, pushing the deis/example-php project or any other projects that use the slugbuilder doesn't work.

From the example-php directory
```
$ git push deis master
Counting objects: 198, done.
Delta compression using up to 4 threads.
Compressing objects: 100% (145/145), done.
Writing objects: 100% (198/198), 360.78 KiB | 0 bytes/s, done.
Total 198 (delta 47), reused 198 (delta 47)
remote: -----> PHP app detected
remote:
remote:        !     WARNING: No composer.json found.
remote:        Using index.php to declare PHP applications is considered legacy
remote:        functionality and may lead to unexpected behavior.
remote:
remote:
remote:        !     ERROR: Failed to resolve runtime requirements.
remote:        If the issue persists, please contact support.
remote:
remote: Slugbuilder returned error code
To ssh://git@deis.local.deisapp.com:2222/example-php.git
 * [new branch]      master -> master
```

So i logged into the coreos server that host the deis-builder and the error is the same : 

```
core@flo-deis-01 ~ $ nse deis-builder
root@2ad55a2a747a:/home/git/example-php.git# git archive master | docker run -i -a stdin -a stdout deis/slugbuilder
-----> PHP app detected

       !     WARNING: No composer.json found.
       Using index.php to declare PHP applications is considered legacy
       functionality and may lead to unexpected behavior.


       !     ERROR: Failed to resolve runtime requirements.
       If the issue persists, please contact support.
```

I think it fails installing buildpacks during boot from https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/builder/slugbuilder/builder/install-buildpacks because the proxy isnt configured.

I'll try building a custom slugbuilder to make it works, but i believe there should be an easier way. Also a better error message :D.

issue_title: Drastic increase in training time when using item/user features
issue_body:
I've spent the last couple of weeks working to improve our recommender, which uses your library under the hood, with the main goal being to include user features at training and prediction time.

My first step was to upgrade to LightFM 1.15 (previously we were on 1.09), so as to make use of `LightFM.Dataset` to replace our previously hand-rolled dataset generation.

We have ~8,000,000 users and ~5,500 items. Users have 13 features, and items have ~200 features.

Plugging this data into `LightFM.Dataset` gives me these datasets to use

- `interactions` (7954774, 5501)
- `weights` (7954774, 5501)
- `item_features` (5501, 5705) (with identity matrix)
- `user_features` (7954774, 13) (without identity matrix)

If I build and train a model in the following way, it takes approximately 15 minutes on my MacBook (a 2015 2.9 GHz Intel Core i5 MacBook Pro with 8Gb of RAM)

```python
model = lightfm.LightFM(no_components=32, loss='warp')
model.fit(
    interactions,
    sample_weight=weights,
    epochs=20,
    num_threads=4,
    verbose=True
)
```

However, when including the `item_features` and `user_features`, it takes approximately 30 minutes to do **one** epoch, let alone the full 20 (again, on my MacBook).

```python
model = lightfm.LightFM(no_components=32, loss='warp')
model.fit(
    interactions,
    sample_weight=weights,
    item_features=item_features,
    user_features=user_features,
    epochs=20,
    num_threads=4,
    verbose=True
)
```

This issue was flagged when I first deployed the above code to production. Our trainer runs on an AWS `c5.2xlarge` EC2 instance, and after it had been running for 4 hours we decided to kill it and revert the change, as it was going to impact some other overnight tasks.

Is this increase in training time to be expected, given these hyperparams and datasets, or is likely that I've made a mistake in my understanding of how LightFM should be used?

One final thing to note: I'm not a data scientist - I've taken ownership of this recommender from a colleague who has since left the company. I understand the Python side of things, but not the nuts and bolts of what your library is actually doing.

I'm trying to convert existing `requirements.txt` file with:
```
pipenv install -r requirements.txt
```

##### Describe your environment

1. Fedora 26
1. Python version: 3
1. Pipenv version: version 8.3.2

##### Expected result

Everything to be installed without errors

##### Actual result

It raises an error:
```
ValueError: Invalid fragment string egg=django-user-clipboard==0.6.1
```

##### Steps to replicate

You need to have requirements.txt file which will have URL entries pointing to package archive file but ending with `#egg=<package-name>==<package.version>`

For example:
```
https://github.com/IndustriaTech/django-user-clipboard/archive/0.6.1.zip#egg=django-user-clipboard==0.6.1
```

If you try to convert this `requirements.txt` file to `Pipfile` with `pipenv install -r requirements.txt` it will fail.

issue_title: Support multiple wheel files in repair command
issue_body: Currently ``auditwheel repair`` command supports only one ``WHEEL_FILE`` argument, I thought it would be nice if it will be possible to give multiple wheels to repair, what do you think? It will be useful in my use case when I build a set of wheels some of which should be repaired.

Setuptools’ setup.py adds a link for ``certifi`` to ``dependency_links``.  The link it adds (``https://pypi.python.org/packages/source/c/certifi/certifi-2016.9.26.tar.gz``) is broken (404.)

I'm not totally sure what's going on here.  My guess is that PyPI has changed how downloads are filed.  (Older packages seem to still be available under ``/packages/source``, but recently released packages appear not to be.)

In any case, this breaks ``zc.buildout`` — at least for me.  Buildout picks up the broken dependency link from the setuptools egg and uses it (unsuccessfully) if an attempt is made to install ``certifi==2016.9.26``.

Hi,

using 'img-retina' mixin, it will create a nested media query for retina devices only.

However, in IE8, respond.js would stop parsing the css file as soon as it meets that nested query, leading to some major issues such as potentially entire page not looking great on desktop (if say, you used retina mixin somewhere in the top of your code).

I had to manually move all my retina css to the bottom of my SCSS file so they will be parsed last and respond.js won't mess things up. 

Any advice on how to tackle nested media queries?

issue_title: "@file support": should handle the BOM on file loading
issue_body:
Otherwise, using `@<parameter-file>` will return an invalid json string for the command to use. Since json file is the main file type, so the core layer should handle it.
CLI already have [the logic](https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/master/src/azure-cli-core/azure/cli/core/_util.py#L91) to handle BOM, so we just need to incorporate the same mechanism to [here](https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/master/src/azure-cli-core/azure/cli/core/application.py#L244)

issue_title: [DNS] Change 'az network dns record update-soa' to 'az network dns record soa update'
issue_body:
Under 'az network dns record', we have a bunch of subgroups (one for each record type) followed by a one-off command 'update-soa'.

The SOA record set is a bit of a special case, in that it can be edited but doesn't support add/remove.

However, I think there's a cleaner way to support this. Remove the 'update-soa' command, and move it to an 'update' command under the 'soa' command group

That way, under 'records' we have a clean list of command groups, one for each record type including SOA. Under each record type, we support add/remove commands, except for SOA which supports an update command instead.

issue_title: Resource list -g implies the resource group exists when it does not
issue_body:
If I run `az resource list -g {group}` and the resource does not exist, I do not get an error that the resource group does not exist. Instead I get a response of:
`[]`
This implies to me that the group exists but has no resources. 

This may be a side effect of PR #2117.

issue_title: Version of kubectl.exe that the command `az acs kubernetes install-cli` brings is incorrect
issue_body:
Hello,
Correct for today, executing CLI command `az acs kubernetes install-cli` downloads the `1.4.5` of the `kubectl.exe` while the server version is `1.5.3`. As result, many commands of the kubernetes CLI do not work (see [issue 392 in azure-engine](https://github.com/Azure/acs-engine/issues/392)).
Please, update the `kubectl.exe` binary with the latest (1.5) version.
The above issue has a link to 1.5.0 binary location.

issue_title: Resource show fails on resources with parentheses in the name
issue_body:
### Description
Command `az resource show` with a resource name that includes parentheses returns "--resource-type was unexpected at this time" in PowerShell 5.0 console.

### Repro Steps

* Open PowerShell console
* Run `az resource show -g "TestGroup" --name "ResourceName(NestedForSomeReason)" --resource-type "Microsoft.OperationsManagement/solutions"` (resource type is example)

Result:

```
--resource-type was unexpected at this time.
C:\WINDOWS\system32>  "C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft SDKs\Azure\CLI2\wbin\\..\python.exe" -Im azure.cli resource show
 -g TestGroup --name ResourceName(NestedForSomeReason) --resource-type Microsoft.OperationsManagement/solutions
```

### Remarks

Parentheses are currently allowed (as far as I know) in resource names, so the CLI should be able to handle them. I know that the parentheses should probably be escaped somehow, but the CLI does not allow for parentheses to be escaped. Neither PowerShell escaping with ``--name "ResourceName`(NestedForSomeReason`)"`` nor python escaping with `--name "ResourceName\(NestedForSomeReason\)"` works.

---

### Environment summary

**Install Method:** How did you install the CLI? (e.g. pip, interactive script, apt-get, Docker, MSI, nightly)  
Answer here: MSI

**CLI Version:** What version of the CLI and modules are installed? (Use `az --version`)  
Answer here: 2.0.12

**OS Version:** What OS and version are you using?  
Answer here: Windows 10 Enterprise

**Shell Type:** What shell are you using? (e.g. bash, cmd.exe, Bash on Windows)  
Answer here: PowerShell 5.1.14

issue_title: Subscription ID is not removed from request or some response bodies
issue_body:
Not, I think, for the same reason as #16, but it might make sense to fix both simultaneously.

This issue is actually two related problems with slightly different solutions:
* The subscription ID isn't removed from the request body because `process_request` only replaces the `uri` field.
* It's not removed from the response body because `_replace_subscription_id`'s regex substitution is case sensitive: it searches for `/subscriptions/([^/]+/` but sometimes it appears in the URI as `/Subscriptions/...`.

issue_title: ServicePrincinpalCredentials() and UserPassCredentials() do not change auth_endpoint
issue_body:
Hi there,

While I was trying to connect to different Azure instances I discovered that the hints from http://azure-sdk-for-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/multicloud.html to connect to different clouds than the default do not work. This is because the functions _https() or more specifically _build_url() always use the string from self._auth_endpoint for URL creation, which is the URL for AzureUSA.

This results in AzureUSA-only connects. It would be cool to see an implementation that can use all different azure instances.

Regards

issue_title: Include checksums for File objects in caching
issue_body:
Our caching mechanisms currently have no means for checking if file objects have been updated between runs. We are bound to run into pipelines that will involve large files so we should have means to support those as well:

1. Support checksums to identify file updates
2. Support for using last modified filestamps as a lightweight method for large files.
3. Include checksums in the signature calculation to allow for re-execution when any inputs have been updated.

issue_title: RFE: Check for invalid escape sequences
issue_body:
In CPython 3.6, invalid escape sequences are deprecated (https://bugs.python.org/issue27364). For example:
```
$ python -W all
Python 3.6.3 (default, Oct 24 2017, 14:48:20) 
[GCC 7.2.0] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> 'C:\Windows\System32'
<stdin>:1: DeprecationWarning: invalid escape sequence \W
'C:\\Windows\\System32'
```
It would be great to check for them.

Ref https://github.com/PyCQA/pyflakes/issues/147

In many cases, unused function arguments are bugs. As such detection behavior would be inappropriate in Pyflakes per the above, the best place to implement such a rule would be here, analogous to the existing B007.

Internally, we use the smarkets style, but with the addition of an import group for internal (company) pip distributed packages. Similar to what is already available for the google style, renamed appnexus.

Could we have support for `application-import-names` and `application-package-names` in either the smarkets style or a new smarknexus style? 

Happy to submit a PR if this is something people are interested.

issue_body:

issue_title: Sorting of json files in BLAST/DIAMOND alignments should also mean FASTA file names get sorted
issue_body:
There's a discrepancy in our treatment of `json.bz2` and `.fasta` files in `noninteractive-alignment-panel.py`. The json file names are sorted in both `dark/blast/alignments.py` and `dark/diamond/alignments.py`. OTOH, the FASTA files are handed to `FastaReads` (or `FastqReads`) by `noninteractive-alignment-panel.py` and are not sorted.

This could cause a problem. I think it would be caught due to non-corresponding read ids as the pairs of json, fasta files are read.  One way to solve it would be to allow a `--sortFilenames` command line arg and pass that to `BlastReadsAlignments` or `DiamondReadsAlignments` and also to the `FastaReads` and `FastqReads` constructors.  That's somehow a lot of work.

The reason we had the sorting in the first place was because we were chopping up FASTA to make many BLAST runs and htcondor was using unpaded numeric strings which didn't sort properly. So we sorted the json output files to make sure the order of their records matched the ids found in the single FASTA file (which had been split but we were giving the unsplit original to `FastaReads`). Now we allow multiple FASTA files and multiple JSON output files. We either need to sort all names or sort none of them, but not do what we're currently doing (only sorting the json).

This hasn't been a problem because we've recently had just one json file or else files with names that do not have a leading number (in which case the stable Python sort leaves the order the same).

issue_title: Package versions with features are not correctly resolved
issue_body:
Initially, I thought this was conda's intended behavior, but now I suspect it is a bug. Suppose I built packages 
```
track_vc11-1.0-0.tar.bz2
foo-1.0-0.tar.bz2
foo-1.0-vc11.tar.bz2
foo-2.0-0.tar.bz2
use_foo-1.0-vc11.tar.bz2
```
from the following `meta.yaml` files:
```yaml
package:
  name:  track_vc11
  version: "1.0"
build:
  features:
    - vc11
  track_features:
    - vc11
```
```yaml
package:
  name:  foo
  version: "1.0"
```
```yaml
package:
  name:  foo
  version: "1.0"
build:
  string: vc11
  features:
    - vc11
requirements:
  build:
    - track_vc11
  run:
    - track_vc11
```
```yaml
package:
  name:  foo
  version: "2.0"
```
```yaml
package:
  name:  use_foo
  version: "1.0"
build:
  string: vc11
  features:
    - vc11
requirements:
  build:
    - track_vc11
    - foo
  run:
    - track_vc11
    - foo
```
Now I create an environment where `vc11` is being tracked:
```
conda create --use-local -n vc11 track_vc11
activate vc11
```
Within this environment, I install `use_foo` and get:
```
The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    foo:     2.0-0
    use_foo: 1.0-vc11 [vc11]
```
However, I expected the output to be
```
    foo:     1.0-vc11 [vc11]
    use_foo: 1.0-vc11 [vc11]
```
because only `foo-1.0-vc11.tar.bz2` has the required feature `vc11`. I digged a bit into the code and found the suspicious statement ([resolve.py#477](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/resolve.py#L477))
```python
            dists = self.get_dists(specs, max_only=True)
```
When I changed this into
```python
            dists = self.get_dists(specs, max_only=False)
```
I got the desired output.

Currently `conda` hardcodes `#!/bin/bash`. If instead `#!/usr/bin/env bash` would be used, then the version of the current environment would be used.

I'm attempting to package `conda` at the moment for an OS where `#!/bin/bash` is unavailable, but `#!/usr/bin/env bash` does work.

The same by the way applies to some of the packages offered via `conda`.

Edit:
I can understand if such change wouldn't be made since there might be distributions where exactly that option isn't available.
The gcc version installed by conda on windows (through mingw) is still 4.7. Current gcc release is 5.2, a version bump to at least gcc 4.9 would be sooo nice!

issue_title: Conda packages should contain authoritative dependency information
issue_body:
A package's `meta.yaml` or its equivalent should be embedded into every package.  I should be able to look at a package tarball and find out _exactly_ what its dependencies are.  Right now that isn't the case.  In fact the situation is (I would argue) worse than that because it _appears_ that the dependencies are embedded, but what is in there is not, in fact, the "real" details of the package dependency.  I still don't understand what the `requires` and `info.json` files contain, but I'm told that the definitive dependencies live elsewhere in a `repodata.json` file that is managed on the package server but not (easily? at all?) available to the conda user who has fetched the package.

A concrete discussion of this can be found here with lots of examples:

https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues/issues/563

issue_title: Warn and don't use expired auth tokens
issue_body:
Does an auth token have its own expiry information built-in?  If so, conda shouldn't attach expired auth tokens to any Anaconda Repository instance: doing so, today, will result in `401 Unauthorized` errors even when trying to access public content.

Instead a warning should be issued and, I believe, conda should proceed without the expired token.

On the server-side when there is a request for a public channel or public asset I would argue the auth hand-shake results should be ignored -- the content is public and the request would succeed with no auth token at all.  But that isn't a conda issue, and I've submitted an enhancement request to the Anaconda Server private repo here: https://github.com/Anaconda-Platform/anaconda-server/issues/4152

It is possible that this is actually an issue to be logged to the https://github.com/Anaconda-Platform/anaconda-client repo, but given that I've only seen the behavior when executing `conda`, and that is also how others have seen it (see #3399 and #5055) I think it makes sense, in the first instance, to log this here.

OS: Windows.

When updating an environment, a snapshot of the environment get stored in the ```[miniconda]\pkgs\.trash```

Is there a way to clean up this trash directory with a ```conda``` command?

issue_title: Consider allowing conda to install python sdists
issue_body:
There isn't a technical reason I know of that conda shouldn't be able to install non-compiler-invoking universal-type python sdist packages with well-formed setup.py files. We'd probably want to follow the pip example here, where pip downloads the sdist, creates a wheel locally, archives it in a cache, and then installs the wheel.

So this proposal requires conda-build along with conda. I think the flow would be for conda to ask conda-build to download the sdist, build a conda package for it, and then conda can install it from the local channel. 

This wouldn't work 100% of the time. It probably wouldn't work reliably for any packages that have extension modules/invoke a compiler. Support for the various python versions also adds extra complexity, so an initial implementation might target this only for packages that are "universal" python packages.

While it won't work 100% of the time, it might work 70% or 80% of the time. And for that extra 20%-30%, most of those are probably the "hard" packages that we've spent building up in repo.continuum.io anyway.

issue_title: source activate sometimes does not empty hash cache in zsh
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...**
  - [X] bug report
  - [ ] feature request


## Current Behavior
<!-- What actually happens?
     If you want to include console output, please use "Steps to Reproduce" below. -->

I have conda 4.3.30.

Sometimes I've noticed when activating an environment that executable locations are not updated to refer to the current environment's bin directory. For example, I just created a new environment and installed the notebook package. `which jupyter-notebook` still gave me a system notebook. I had to empty the zsh hash cache with `hash -r` for zsh to recognize that the conda environment had a `jupyter-notebook` that took precedence over the system one.

My understanding is that resetting the PATH *should* empty the cache, but apparently something didn't work here. I wonder if conda should do `hash -r` when starting a new environment to make sure the cache is empty.

I talked with @fperez once about how conda activates new environments, and he mentioned he had a system where he *always* started a subshell with the new environment. That seems to be a simple solution to cleanly starting and exiting environments.

 '//anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/IPython/extensions',
 '/Users/mudy/.ipython']
```

How to fix this?

In some cases, `conda.install.find_new_location()` returns the path that is not writable to the current process. This occurs for me as follows:

1. Install conda.
2. Switch to another user, who has read but not write permission to the conda installation.
3. Configure a channel other than repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free that contains some of the same packages, _e.g._ a local mirror.
4. Try create a new environment.

I think what happens is that the `urls.txt` entries for the existing packages show `repo.continuum.io` URLs, which don't match the current channel URLs, so conda tries to download and unpack the packages again.  This confuses `find_new_location()`, which finds the package in the `package_cache`, but the directory is not writable.

I think one or two `try_write()` checks in `find_new_location()` work around this.

More broadly, if I understand correctly, `urls.txt` uses the URL to identify packages, which isn't really right if the same package might be available from more than one place.  Using the package m5sum or something similar might be better?

issue_title: Feature request: ability to expose system installed files as installable conda packages
issue_body:
We would like the ability to be able to "install" system files into a conda-based python environment.

Specifically, Parallel Studio has large sets of libraries that can be used with python (MKL, DAAL, TBB, etc.). We would like the ability to expose these to a conda-based python environment so a user could "conda install" the libraries *without copying them*. Because they are, combined, hundreds of MB, the solution must avoid creating copies (where possible).

Requirements:
- The local library packages look like regular conda packages so they can satisfy requirements of other conda packages (such as numpy requiring MKL) and behave in a consistent manner when "install", "update" and "remove" commands are given
- The local libraries should be able to maintain their own availability. In other words, when the system installation changes, it should be able to update its availability for python users
- The local library package must be able to enforce version consistency with the software version it references

Proactive discovery of local resources by the python installation is not a requirement for our use case.

issue_title: Google GeocoderQuotaExceeded Though API Console doesn't show exceeding usage
issue_body:
Hi all,

Trying to geocode 55 records, and only getting 1/2way through before I get the error:

_geopy.exc.GeocoderQuotaExceeded: The given key has gone over the requests limit in the 24 hour period or has submitted too many requests in too short a period of time._

Looking at my Google Developers Console, the API usage is nowhere near the limit (i'm only < 100 per day) and the requests / second aren't near the limit either.

Is there a middle man here that I can't see that is causing this error? Is GeoPy sending requests too fast?

Thanks!

Due to the release of the new Fabric 2.0, the old API is no longer supported.

We must review it and update the fabric tasks.

issue_title: MemoryBuffer should manage its memory more automatically
issue_body:
Currently we store `MemoryBuffer*` objects everywhere, which is prone to memory leaks.

Instead, it is better to have `MemoryBuffer` a wrapper object, containing `MemoryBufferImpl*` pointer that it manages. The semantics should be similar to that of `std::shared_pointer`: allow `MemoryBuffer`s to be shallow-copyable, and destroy the underlying `Impl*` objects automatically when their reference count reaches 0.

        consumers = [test_consumer1, test_consumer2, .., ..]
        pool_size = len(consumers)
        pool = mp.Pool(pool_size)

        try:
            for consumer in consumers:
                pool.apply_async(consumer)

          for i in range(5):

                    channel=self.channel,

                    max_in_flight=5,

我要模拟目录的实现,期望的是能够返回指定前缀作为父目录的直属子目录和文件.
前缀为GYyMYR/images/,分隔符为"/"的应该是GYyMYR/images/20160407/的形式,也就是按日期归类的.
因此下面的 list 操作不会有实际文件的key,应该都是类似目录的.

bucket.list(bucket_name, prefix="GYyMYR/images/", marker=marker, limit=100, delimiter="/")

GYyMYR/images/20160407/
GYyMYR/images/20160411/
GYyMYR/images/20160807/

bucket.list(bucket_name, prefix="GYyMYR/images/", marker=marker, limit=1, delimiter="/")

bucket.list(bucket_name, prefix="GYyMYR/images/", marker=marker, limit=2, delimiter="/")

GYyMYR/images/20160407/

这应该是服务端的问题

The dynamical part of the weights computation to guaranty positive definiteness is missing (around line 2819).

The current code either seeds `np.random` with a user specified seed, or a new random seed:

https://github.com/CMA-ES/pycma/blob/master/cma/evolution_strategy.py#L1359-L1364

This means that if the user seeds `np.random`, then generates random samples, then calls cma, and then generates more samples, the code still produces arbitrary output.

A workaround is to always pass a seed to cma, but it seems to me that changing the state of `numpy.random` (unless asked specifically) is a side-effect that should be eliminated

issue_title: Test publish yum repo with different checksums
issue_body:
1. create an "rpm" repo in pulp with one or more RPMs, SRPMs, and DRPMs.
2. publish it with the "sha256" checksum type
3. verify publish (see below)
4. publish it with the "sha1" checksum type
5. verify publish (see below)
### Verification

It depends on how fancy and robust you want to get when it comes to inspecting the published XML. You'll need to somehow look at primary, other, filelists, and prestodelta XML files and make sure all of the unit-related checksums are using the checksum type you asked for.

Smash could download the files and actually parse them as XML. That wouldn't be too difficult, but who likes parsing XML...

Smash could just ensure that the string "sha256" shows up in the case when it should, and doesn't when it shouldn't.

In the case of "other" and "filelists", the checksum type itself won't be included. Each package will have a `pkgid` attribute that is the checksum value of the specified type. You could just verify that it's the correct length. Or you could actually cross-reference it with the checksum for the corresponding package listed in primary.

Another general way to help with this verification is to just make sure yum and/or dnf can actually read the metadata and use it. In my manual testing, I published a rhel7 kickstart repo and kickstarted a VM from it, just to make sure it was generally sane.

No error message. The program hangs at the self.mod.predict(it) line on the second time execute() is called. Recreating the module every time will not cause it to hang but should be unnecessary. Calling it in a loop over a collection of separate images will not cause it to hang.

class RefineNet(object):

        seg_net_prefix = os.path.join(SNAPSHOT_FOLDER, MODEL_NAME)
        self.arg_dict, self.aux_dict, _ = misc.load_checkpoint(seg_net_prefix, self.epoch, load_symbol=False)

        seg_net = create_infer(CLASSNUM, WORKSPACE)
        self.mod = mx.module.Module(seg_net, data_names=('data',), label_names=(), context=self.ctx)

        t0 = datetime.datetime.now()

        t0 = datetime.datetime.now()
        self.mod.bind(data_shapes=[("data", (1, 3, 640, 640))], for_training=False, grad_req='null')
        t1 = datetime.datetime.now()
        print('module reset bind took %s' % (t1 - t0))

        t0 = datetime.datetime.now()
        it = mx.io.NDArrayIter(data = [mx.nd.array(im)])
        t1 = datetime.datetime.now()

Recreating the entire module object every time solves it but introduces about a second of computation time that should be unnecessary. I've narrowed it down to a call to mod.predict(), mod.forward(), or mod.bind(force_reset = True).

issue_title: Discussion and troubleshooting on PyPI (pip) installation
issue_body:
PyPI for [Apache MXNet](http://mxnet.apache.org) installation has been available for a while and has been the most important channel for distribution. I'm opening this issue to collect feedbacks, suggestions and issue reports for releases and nightly pre-releases.

## Current Status
Supported variants:
- [mxnet](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/mxnet/) on `Windows`, `Linux`, and `Mac OSX`, with CPU-only without MKL2017 support.
- [mxnet-mkl](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/mxnet-mkl/) on both `Linux` and `Mac OSX`, with CPU-only `MKL2017` support (with MKL experimental).
- [mxnet-cu75](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/mxnet-cu75/) on `Linux`, supports `CUDA-7.5`.
- [mxnet-cu80](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/mxnet-cu80/) on `Windows` and `Linux`, supports `CUDA-8.0`.
- [mxnet-cu90](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/mxnet-cu90/) on `Windows` and `Linux`, supports `CUDA-9.0`.
- [mxnet-cu75mkl](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/mxnet-cu75mkl/) on `Linux`, supports `CUDA-7.5` and `MKL2017` (with MKL experimental).
- [mxnet-cu80mkl](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/mxnet-cu80mkl/) on `Linux`, supports `CUDA-8.0` and `MKL2017` (with MKL experimental).
- [mxnet-cu90mkl](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/mxnet-cu90mkl/) on `Linux`, supports `CUDA-9.0` and `MKL2017` (with MKL experimental).

(Note: MKL-DNN integration is in progress will likely replace MKL2017.)

## Usage
### Release
For release, users can install it via
```bash
MXNET_VARIANT=mxnet-cu90 MXNET_VERSION=0.12.0 pip install --upgrade ${MXNET_VARIANT}==${MXNET_VERSION}
```

Be sure to update `pip` (`setuptools`) to the most recent version, by `pip install --upgrade pip`.

### Nightly
For nightly pre-release, users can install it via `pip install --pre --upgrade`. Be sure to update `pip` (`setuptools`) to the most recent version, by `pip install --upgrade pip`.

## Report issue
For bug submissions on issues found in the pre-releases, please attach:
- MXNet pip package version
- Environment report:
```
What to do:
1. Download the diagnosis script from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/master/tools/diagnose.py
2. Run the script using `python diagnose.py` and paste its output here.

```
- Problem code
  - Code snippet to reproduce the issue
  - Code should use dummy data (e.g. `mx.nd.random.uniform`), unless bug is only triggered with specific data value, in which case that specific value should be attached.

The concat operator takes the argument `dim` to specify along which axis to concat. How do you all feel about changing the argument name to `axis`? Almost every operator in mxnet uses the key-word name `axis` to specify this information and it's a little hard to remember that it's `dim` for concat.

It seems the current implementation of `concat` operator is based on mshadow. And if the input of concat has multiple NDArray, on gpu, it will launch kernel for many times. Tensorflow has customized kernel for concat operator, it will do kernel launch only once. 
Any plan to optimize this?

issue_title: Feature request: please speed up asnumpy()

Dear all, 

I am trying to visualize some predictions from an NN run so I need to use ```asnumpy()``` to get ```imshow``` visualizations. issue_title: Queue put-get delay of many seconds?
issue_body: I'm using billiard to run two processes (a & b) in parallel with a pair of Queue()s to communicate between them (a->b and b->a) and despite using a loop in each process that should be checking the queues every few milliseconds, I'm experiencing a delay of up to many seconds between one process putting a message in a queue and the other process receiving it. Frustratingly, it seems to be variable as to when these delays manifest, with delays of only 10-100ms otherwise. Any suggestions for troubleshooting something like this?

issue_title: use correct ssl version with urllib3 and pyopenssl
issue_body:
There are situations where ``get_max_tls_protocol()`` will get a max openssl version from python ``ssl`` that is higher than what ``urllib3`` supports.

The error will be:

```
KeyError: 5
```
in ``requests/packages/urllib3/contrib/pyopenssl.py:271`` on the line with ``ctx = OpenSSL.SSL.Context(_openssl_versions[ssl_version])``

Using the following replacement for ``docker.ssladapter.ssladapter.get_max_tls_protocol()`` worked for me, but there might be other cases I am missing:

```python
from requests.packages.urllib3.contrib import pyopenssl
def get_max_tls_protocol():
    protocols = ('PROTOCOL_TLSv1_2',
                 'PROTOCOL_TLSv1_1',
                 'PROTOCOL_TLSv1',
    for proto in protocols:
        if hasattr(ssl, proto):
            proto_id = getattr(ssl, proto)
            if proto_id in pyopenssl._openssl_versions:
                return proto_id
```

issue_title: Paginated Calls Don't Error Until Iteration Time
issue_body:
There's a somewhat unexpected/odd behavior that results from the way that Python handles iterator definition.

Consider the following simple example:
```python
>>> def err_iter():
...     raise ValueError()
...     for x in (1, 2):
...             yield x
... 
>>> xs = err_iter() # fine, but will error when we try to use it
>>> next(xs) # uh-oh!
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "<stdin>", line 2, in err_iter
ValueError
```

It's a bit of a subtlety of the way that iterator functions work -- it doesn't run at all until the first call to `next()` happens. That holds even if it will error before ever reaching the `yield`.

For paginated API calls, the behavior is unintuitive and confusing.
Even an unauthenticated call to instantiate the iterator will "work". It will just fail at iteration time.

It would be better to raise these errors at iteration instantiation time.
I think we can do that by attaching a little bit of state to the class instance in the PaginatedResource decorator.

How to reproduce:
1. Create new stack
1. Deploy
1. Fails with error

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/saga/Envs/py27/bin/kumo", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/Users/saga/Envs/py27/lib/python2.7/site-packages/gcdt/kumo_main.py", line 79, in main
    print_parameter_diff(boto_session, conf)
  File "/Users/saga/Envs/py27/lib/python2.7/site-packages/gcdt/kumo_core.py", line 72, in print_parameter_diff
    for param in stack.parameters:
TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not iterable
```

Fix:
Check if `stack.parameters is not None` in kumo_core.py before iterating over the variable.
![brotli](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/203457/24612601/07e41f72-1886-11e7-83e7-0a573315d266.png)

issue_body:
trade_type: MWEB
缺少回调参数：mweb_url

We need to be able to specify things like tile blocksizes and compression for output images. 

Check out https://github.com/mapbox/nodata/blob/master/nodata/scripts/cli.py and the use of `from rasterio.rio.options import creation_options`

For compatibility with [`mapbox-upload`](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-upload), the current upload CLI arguments follow suit. However, we might want to consider tweaking the design a bit

1. `mapbox upload DESTINATION-tileset and SOURCE-file` - the current ordering is opposite what one would expect from unix tools like `cp` which generally follow the `cmd source destination` pattern. Switch 'em? 
1. we should consider making `--name` default to the basename of the file rather than None. This avoids having a bunch of "Untitled" tilesets in studio should you forget to supply the optional name.
1. we should also consider making the username in the tileset id optional. We can infer the username from the access token and prepend it if there is no username supplied. This would help to write more reusable bash scripts intended to be run with different accounts.

Should we implement these changes? If so, do they belong here or could some be baked into SDK behavior?
The Data API isn't on https://www.mapbox.com/developers/api/ but let's get it in the module at preview time.

issue_title: Add function to load file directly for convenience
issue_body:
Every time I want to load a yaml file, I have to either:

1. Write this ugly and complicated script:

```javascript
import * as yaml from 'js-yaml'
import * as fs from 'fs'
const object = yaml.safeLoad(fs.readFileSync('file.yaml', 'utf8'))
```

2. Use an intermediate [module](https://github.com/linusu/load-yaml-file) which unnecessarily pollutes `package.json`:

```javascript
import * as yaml from 'load-yaml-file'
const object = yaml.sync('file.yaml')
```
So I wish js-yaml just provide these little functions for sake of developers' convenience and laziness:

```javascript
import * as yaml from 'js-yaml'
yaml.loadFile('file.yaml') // -> Promise
yaml.safeLoadFile('file.yaml') // -> Promise
yaml.loadFileSync('file.yaml') // -> Parsed result
yaml.safeLoadFileSync('file.yaml') // -> Parsed result
```
I noticed your [argparse](https://www.npmjs.com/package/argparse) dep was using `~0.1.11`. Thought ya might dig [they](https://github.com/nodeca/argparse) just bumped to 1.0.0.

issue_title: With persistent connections, opbeat can cause an additional error when collecting user data
issue_body:
With Django's persistent connections, it can happen that a connection to the database is closed for misc. reasons. This will cause an `OperationalError` in Django. When Opbeat tries to pick up the user information for that error, it tries accessing the database, causing an additional `OperationalError`.

Solution for now is to catch any `OperationalError`s when we're gathering the extra data (user info etc.) and not bubble it up further. It would be nice if we instead added a note to the data that we were unable to collect it before it's sent.

cc @apollo13

issue_title: No way to fix resources in "pending delete" status which cannot be deleted without property changes
issue_body:
When a resource fails to delete and gets marked as "pending delete", there is no longer any way to modify the properties on the resource.  It can be the case that there is no way to delete the resources without modifying a proeprty on the resource.  This leads to it being impossible to make progress with a stack unless manual edits are made to the checkpoint via `pulumi stack export`/`pulumi stack import`.

See https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/issues/1596 for an example of this, with `aws.rds.Instance` failing to delete due to:

```
error: Plan apply failed: deleting urn:pulumi:twitch::webserver::aws:rds/instance:Instance::baseA: DB Instance FinalSnapshotIdentifier is required when a final snapshot is required
```

This error cannot be fixed (I believe) without modifying properties on the resource in the checkpoint file.

Is there an easy way to defer to the existing STL type converters, maybe with a trivial converter so I don't need to duplicate existing code?

Dear Nathaniel,

What do you think it would take to implement support for mixed effects formulas (parsing and design matrix construction) as they are used in R's `lme4`? In terms of effort, does it appear to you like it could be easily achieved by composing/reusing existing `patsy` features, or is it way more involved?

Currently the array space for temporaries is `PyMalloc`ed individually for each thread once per call to `NumExpr_run()`.  A faster solution would be to take the approach of NumPy and have a pre-allocated pool that could be assigned for the temporaries for each thread by a manager, hooked into the global state.  Such a system would also be easier to verify that it does not leak memory.

At present `NPY_MAX_ARGS` == 32, so the maximum space that could be allocated to temporaries would be approximately `32*4096*N_THREADS*ITEMSIZE`, so for 8 threads and `float64` the pre-allocated space would be a maximum of 8.4 MB of RAM.

issue_title: authorization failing for OAuth GET functions after successful POST call
issue_body:
after calling a function that uses POST, for example `client.like(...)` or `client.create_text(...)`, the next call to a 'GET' function requiring OAuth authorization fails with a 401 error (calling GET functions that require an API key work fine however).

for example after the POST function `client.create_text(...)`, a call to the GET function` client.likes(...)` will fail, although a call to the GET function `client.posts(...)` will work fine. `client.likes(...)` requires OAuth whereas `client.posts(...)` requires an API key -- according to the [tumblr API documentation](https://www.tumblr.com/docs/en/api/v2/).

don't know if this is an issue with pytumblr, the oauth library, or the tumblr API itself. a workaround is to reinitialize the client before every call with: `client = pytumblr.TumblrRestClient(...)`

If we have a workflow with a task executed on remote host `on_host`. Then if the same workflow is executed locally on the host, `on-host` should not do anything. The problem is that it is not always easy to tell if the host name match....

There is also the case (at least for testing) and we set `on_host=localhost` with `path_map` to move data to another location to run.

issue_title: Error message for %run -r only appears on terminal
issue_body: Not in jupyter notebook.
A quick test on Linux shows that sos does not work well on firefox. More testing is needed.

issue_title: Minitor script or task, or both?
issue_body: We previously use a monitor thread for script execution. With the need to monitor the execution of tasks, the monitor process is moved to tasks. This is generally ok, but misses an important case, namely when the job is executed in docker, the sos process cannot monitor the CPU and memory usage of tasks. Not sure how we can address this problem.

Git control ipynb file is discovered because of embedded graphics etc. A common practice would be export ipynb to sos and track it. We could help this process by

%sossave -c -p -m

Where c for commit m for message and p for push. With another option -a (all for all cells), users can export all cells and push to repository using command

%sossave -acp

issue_title: Add type trait to source code, with assistance from MonkeyType
issue_body:
I have been playing with [MonkeyType](https://github.com/Instagram/MonkeyType) and found it an interesting and usable tool for us to add type trait to our source code.

The easiest way to use MonkeyType is to run `monkeytype run test_???.py` to generate type stubs and apply them to source code. However, due to our particular layout of source code, we should

1. MonkeyType bypass system libraries but our tests test against installed `sos`. To generate type trait, we will have to define a configuration file `test/monkeytype_config.py` with content:

```
from types import CodeType
from monkeytype.tracing import CodeFilter
from monkeytype.config import DefaultConfig, default_code_filter

class MyConfig(DefaultConfig):

    def code_filter(self) -> CodeFilter:
        def my_code_filter(code: CodeType) -> bool:
            return 'mypackage' in code.co_filename or default_code_filter(code)
        return my_code_filter

CONFIG = MyConfig()
```

2. After running tests and generated `monkeytype.sqlite`, we need to copy this file to `sos/src`.
3. Then, go to `sos/src`, run `monkeytype stub sos.MODULE` (e.g. `sos.parser`).

issue_title: Discussion: should we have coverage on tests?
issue_body:
My argument is yes we should. Testing is very important and doubly so that all the test code gets written. Consider the following test:
```python
def test_iter():
    a  = db.restream(hdr)
    for b in a:
        assert something
```
This test will not fail if a is empty, we will simply miss the assert. One way to deal with this is to throw a known fail into the code, run it, check that it failed, then remove it because it is messy. Another way to deal with this would be to run coverage on the test code. Coverage would show that the for loop line was ran but none of the internals were executed thus hinting to us that the internal asserts are not being tested.

One potential solution is to run coverage twice, one without the test code (for our line tallys) and one with the tests covered to test the health of the test codebase.

issue_title: Consumer can't get any messages after reset_offsets
issue_body:
The following code is hangs (on the last consume() call). 

_Case definition: Kafka contains topic `test` with a lot of messages, I'm trying to move offset to some position using the `reset_offsets`. pykafka 2.7.0, Python 3.7.0_
```python
from pykafka import KafkaClient

pyclient = KafkaClient(hosts="localhost:9092")
topic = pyclient.topics[b'test']
c = topic.get_simple_consumer(
    consumer_group=b'mygroup', 
    auto_offset_reset=common.OffsetType.EARLIEST)
c.reset_offsets(partition_offsets=[(c.partitions[0], 10)])
c.commit_offsets()
c.consume()
```
New instances of consumer works as expected (starts from 11 offset).
It looks like a bug. After some manipulations I have found few workarounds:
```python
# ...
c.reset_offsets(partition_offsets=[(c.partitions[0], 10)])
c.commit_offsets()
c.consume(block=False) # return None
c.consume() # now works as expected
```
```python
# ...
c.reset_offsets(partition_offsets=[(c.partitions[0], 10)])
c.commit_offsets()
c.fetch()
c.consume() # now works as expected
```

Specifically, data.py (https://github.com/CCampJr/orange-spectroscopy/blob/h5loader/orangecontrib/spectroscopy/data.py) the class H5Reader (not to be confused with the pre-existing HDF5Reader_HERMES).

issue_title: why revert the batching relevant components?
issue_body: Is there any issue?

Very long values, such as:

```
"ConstraintDescription": "Must be 'foo', 'bar', 'baz', 'foo', 'bar', 'baz', 'foo', 'bar', 'baz', 'foo', 'bar', 'baz'..."
```

Becomes the yaml:

```
Constraint Description: Must be 'foo', 'bar', 'baz', 'foo', 'bar', 'baz', 'foo', 'bar', 'baz',
    foo', 'bar', 'baz'...
```

Note the missing quotation on the second line of the yaml there.

issue_title: Must error when adding an API Event for a path that is not defined in Swagger

issue_body:
When I create a stack that uses the VpcConfig (hard coded due to #22) and then go to delete the stack the resulting ENI that is created to enable the VpcConfig isn't deleted so when I go to delete the database and it's security group, the security group cannot be deleted due to the lingering lambda function's ENI.

As a user, when I delete a `AWS::Serverless::Function` with a `VpcConfig` specified the ENI should be deleted as well.

`AWS::DynamoDB::Table` supports server-side encryption, but this is not an option for `AWS::Serverless::SimpleTable`.  I propose that we add a property to enable it, while it remains disabled by default.

**SAM Template:**

```yaml
MyTable:
  Type: AWS::Serverless::SimpleTable
  Properties:
    ...
    SSESpecification:
      SSEEnabled: boolean
```

**Transformed CFN Template:**

```yaml
MyTable:
  Type: AWS::DynamoDB::Table
  Properties:
    ...
    SSESpecification:
      SSEEnabled: boolean
```

I'm willing to work on this.  I think it's a great first-issue now that the SAM translator is open-source.

issue_title: Stage variable not properly injected into swagger doc
issue_body:
I have the following resources in a SAM CloudFormation template (this template is not complete—just showing the relevant parts):

```yaml
Parameters:
  EncryptedMongoUrl:
    Type: "String"
    Description: "Encrypted MONGO_URL value."
  UserPoolName:
    Type: "String"
    Description: "Desired name of the Cognito user pool."
Resources:
  UserPool:
    Type: "AWS::Cognito::UserPool"
    Properties:
      UserPoolName: !Ref UserPoolName
      Policies:
        PasswordPolicy:
          MinimumLength: 8
          RequireLowercase: true
          RequireUppercase: true
          RequireNumbers: true
          RequireSymbols: false
  ApiGateway:
    Type: "AWS::Serverless::Api"
    DependsOn: UserPool
    Properties:
      StageName: "v1"
      DefinitionUri: "./lambda-proxy-api.yaml"
      Variables:
        GraphQLFuncName: !Ref GraphQL
        UserPoolArn: !GetAtt UserPool.Arn
```

Here's the `lambda-proxy-api.yaml` file:

```yaml
---
swagger: 2.0
info:
  title: OnSpotServerlessApi
paths:
  "/graphql":
    post:
      responses: {}
      security:
      - UserPool: []
      x-amazon-apigateway-integration:
        uri: arn:aws:apigateway:us-east-1:lambda:path/2015-03-31/functions/arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:xxx:function:${stageVariables.GraphQLFuncName}/invocations
        httpMethod: POST
        type: aws_proxy
securityDefinitions:
  UserPool:
    type: apiKey
    name: Authorization
    in: header
    x-amazon-apigateway-authtype: cognito_user_pools
    x-amazon-apigateway-authorizer:
      providerARNs:
      - ${stageVariables.UserPoolArn}
      type: cognito_user_pools
```

Oddly enough, the GraphQL function name comes through just fine, but the `stageVariables.UserPoolArn` does not. When the stack tries to build, I get this error:

>Errors found during import: Unable to create authorizer 'UserPool': ProviderARNs need to be valid Cognito Userpools. Invalid ARNs- ${stageVariables.UserPoolArn} Unable to put method 'POST' on resource at path '/graphql': Invalid authorizer ID specified. Setting the authorization type to CUSTOM or COGNITO_USER_POOLS requires a valid authorizer.

Why does this variable interpolation not work?

Currently we pin specific versions of all our of Python dependencies in `certbot-auto` but don't do this in our PyPI packages. Should we change this? I'd be very interested to see what other popular client side Python projects with OS dependencies do. (I'm not personally aware of any and over the past 2 years I've learned why.)

There was discussion of this in IRC between @erikrose, @ohemorange, and @zjs, but I didn't get a chance to respond while everyone was still in the channel. Thinking about this for a bit, I think I'm against pinning while we have our current distribution methods, but let's see where this helps us.

#### certbot-auto

We already pin specific dependency versions and check its hash.

#### OS packages

While we're currently relying and encouraging systems to package Certbot and for people to use those packages, pinning specific versions of our dependencies isn't feasible. While it'd be nice to only support only one version of our dependencies at a time, the packages available in different distros vary wildly and we need to support multiple versions. Pinning specific versions makes testing different package versions harder for us (see [tox.ini](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/tox.ini#L37) and #4049) and makes OS packaging harder. I see no benefit here.

Pinning `dependency<next.major.version` is interesting. This makes it clear that we don't expect Certbot to work with new major versions of our dependencies. If Certbot or its tests are run with a new dependency version though, it'll definitely fail if we pin, but it may not if we don't. Which behavior do we want?

#### pip install

We never tell anyone to install this way and I am perfectly fine not supporting this installation method as was suggested in IRC. In general, I think Python does a poor job supporting `pip` installing packages with OS dependencies which we have many of. Unless you have an intimate understanding of the project and understand you need to install `libffi-dev`, `gcc`, `libaugeas`, and more (or luckily happen to have those packages installed), `pip install` isn't going to work.

#### developer setup

`tools/venv.sh` failing slows down developers, but:

1. If this is our primary motivation for this, we can fix it without affecting packaging.
2. It's beneficial for us to know when a dependency changes that breaks Certbot (although we could test this during CI or nightly testing).

issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):
Centos 7.3

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
EPEL package certbot.noarch 0.12.0-4.el7

## I ran this command and it produced this output:
systemctl start certbot-renew.service

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:
File `/lib/systemd/system/certbot-renew.service` from package has lines
```
[Service]
EnvironmentFile=-/etc/sysconfig/certbot
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/usr/bin/certbot renew --pre-hook $PRE_HOOK --post-hook $POST_HOOK --renew-hook $RENEW_HOOK $CERTBOT_ARGS
```
Therefore, it rely on `/etc/sysconfig/certbot` to set the variables. Ones are set by default like 
```
PRE_HOOK="''"
```
So, something goes wrong with variables passing, systemd clears all type of quotes and puts nothing instead of ''

If I simulate certbot run with command from shell prompt:
```
/usr/bin/certbot renew --pre-hook '' --post-hook '' --renew-hook ''
```
It is Ok, it works.

The workaround is to put any "do nothing" command in the quotes, for example:
```
PRE_HOOK="'echo >/dev/null'"
```
And the same to all others. Now it works.

## Here is a Certbot log showing the issue (if available):
No cerbot logs created at all.

System messages log shows:
```
May 17 00:00:06 pavlyuts systemd: Starting This service automatically renews any certbot certificates found...
May 17 00:00:06 pavlyuts certbot: usage:
May 17 00:00:06 pavlyuts certbot: certbot [SUBCOMMAND] [options] [-d DOMAIN] [-d DOMAIN] ...

May 17 00:00:07 pavlyuts systemd: certbot-renew.service: main process exited, code=exited, status=2/INVALIDARGUMENT
May 17 00:00:07 pavlyuts systemd: Failed to start This service automatically renews any certbot certificates found.
May 17 00:00:07 pavlyuts systemd: Unit certbot-renew.service entered failed state.
May 17 00:00:07 pavlyuts systemd: certbot-renew.service failed.`
```

I run the following from cron every month, and got this error:

```
/opt/letsencrypt/letsencrypt-auto certonly --quiet --standalone --renew-by-default -d example.org -d www.example.org  
Creating virtual environment...
Installing Python packages...     
Installation succeeded.               
An unexpected error occurred:             
DistributionNotFound: The 'ndg-httpsclient' distribution was not found and is required by requests                               
Please see the logfile 'certbot.log' for more details.
```

Clearly, "example" is not my domain, I used the correct one. The certbot.log does not offer any more details:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/bin/letsencrypt", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/opt/letsencrypt/certbot/main.py", line 858, in main
    plugins = plugins_disco.PluginsRegistry.find_all()
  File "/opt/letsencrypt/certbot/plugins/disco.py", line 183, in find_all
    plugin_ep = PluginEntryPoint(entry_point)
  File "/opt/letsencrypt/certbot/plugins/disco.py", line 34, in __init__
    self.plugin_cls = entry_point.load()
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 2201, in load
    self.require(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 2218, in require
    items = working_set.resolve(reqs, env, installer)
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 830, in resolve
    raise DistributionNotFound(req, requirers)
DistributionNotFound: The 'ndg-httpsclient' distribution was not found and is required by requests
```

What am I missing?

System is Ubuntu 14.04.5 LTS (Trusty Tahr) if that matters and certbot is the lastest master as of today.

Now that #492 has been resolved, I think we should stop using Quay.io entirely. Why?

1. Docker Hub is the conventional place for Docker images.
2. Our current repository there is letsencrypt/letsencrypt and I'm unaware of a way to rename it. I have control of the certbot name, however, I'm unaware of a way to redirect the current repository to certbot.
3. Do we really need both Quay.io and Docker Hub?

Proposal: Change the deprecation warning in `Dockerfile-old` to say that our Dockerfile has been rewritten and moved to Docker Hub. Set up automatic builds so the image there stays up to date. Once we've waited for an extended period of time and/or Quay.io requires active maintenance, we turn off the repository entirely.

Thoughts?

cc @joohoi

issue_title: Can't update or delete registration with account reuse
issue_body:
Create an ACMEv1 account by running:
```
sudo certbot register  --server https://acme-staging.api.letsencrypt.org/directory
```
Then try to delete (or update) the account when using the ACMEv2 server:
```
sudo certbot unregister --server https://acme-staging-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory
```
It will fail with an error message like:
```
The request message was malformed :: No JWK in JWS header
```
This happens without setting the server if you have an ACMEv1 prod account and checkout the `acmev2-default` branch.

This happens because in `acme.client.BackwardsCompatibleClientV2` we're using the ACMEv2 client, but we POST to the URL taken from the registration on disk which is an ACMEv1 URL. This mismatch causes us to send the wrong values in the request causing this error.

issue_title: Network allocation features return the wrong results.
issue_body:
Some quirks popped up while debugging the new CLI features for pynsot.

There's definitely a bug in `next_address` and `next_network` that's causing assigned or reserved addresses to be offered. There also seems to be a related bug where networks are not offered unless they no longer match any potential children, which is also bad.

issue_title: Exception: reindex() got an unexpected keyword argument 'wait_for_active_shards' when running Curator on windows
issue_body:

- Version: 10.0.14393 Build 14393 (from System Information)
- OS Build: 14393.1480 (from About Your PC System Settings)

Test Components:

Description:

Seemingly only affecting Windows, https://artifacts.elastic.co/downloads/elasticsearch/elasticsearch-5.5.1.msiCurator throws a `reindex() got an unexpected keyword argument 'wait_for_active_shards'` error when trying to run a simple reindex with:

```
C:\Users\Peter\Downloads\elasticsearch-curator-5.1.1-amd64\curator-5.1.1-amd64>curator --config ./curator.yml reindex-test.yml

2017-07-31 18:22:54,596 ERROR Failed to complete action: reindex. <class 'curator.exceptions.FailedExecution'>: Exception encountered. Rerun with loglevel DEBUG and/or check Elasticsearch logs for more information. Exception: reindex() got an unexpected keyword argument 'wait_for_active_shards'

  - https://artifacts.elastic.co/downloads/elasticsearch/elasticsearch-5.5.1.msi
- Start Elasticsearch if not already started
- Download and unzip Kibana windows zip and run Kibana:

- Download and extract Curator windows zip package:
  - https://packages.elastic.co/curator/5/windows/elasticsearch-curator-5.1.1-amd64.zip

Create a test index in Elasticsearch (via Kibana dev console or your preferred method):

```
POST /index1/doc/
{
  "message": "this is a test message"
}
```

Create a basic Curator config as `config.yml`:
```
---
# Remember, leave a key empty if there is no value.  None will be a string,
# not a Python "NoneType"
client:
  hosts:
    - 127.0.0.1
  port: 9200
  url_prefix:
  use_ssl: False
  certificate:
  client_cert:
  client_key:
  timeout: 30
  master_only: False

logging:
  loglevel: INFO
  logfile:
  logformat: default
  blacklist: ['elasticsearch', 'urllib3']
```

create an action file based on the simple reindex example in the docs, `reindex-test.yml`:
- https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/ex_reindex.html

```
---
# Remember, leave a key empty if there is no value.  None will be a string,
# not a Python "NoneType"
#
# Also remember that all examples have 'disable_action' set to True.  If you
# want to use this action as a template, be sure to set this to False after
# copying it.
actions:
  1:
    description: "Reindex index1 into index2"
    action: reindex
    options:
      disable_action: False
      wait_interval: 9
      max_wait: -1
      request_body:
        source:
          index: index1
        dest:
          index: index2
    filters:
      - filtertype: none
```

Test with Curator in a  command window using `curator --config ./curator.yml reindex-test.yml`:

```
C:\Users\Peter\Downloads\elasticsearch-curator-5.1.1-amd64\curator-5.1.1-amd64>curator --config ./curator.yml reindex-test.yml
2017-07-31 18:22:54,578 INFO      Preparing Action ID: 1, "reindex"

Other notes:

I'm running python 2.7 connecting to AWS elasticsearch service using the 2.2 release of elasticsearch-py.  To connect I use requests_aws4auth as recommended in your docs (thanks for integrating that!).

When writing to elasticsearch (bulk upload, creating a doc etc) I get this error:

```UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte 0xe2 in position 2102: ordinal not in range(128)```

I know that the library was changed a couple months ago to stop hiding unicode errors but this change coincides with the introduction of support for requests_aws4auth as they are both first seen in the 2.2 release.  Upgrading to python 3 is not an option yet given other dependencies.

I have come up with a workaround for now using a custom serializer that essentially reverts the unicode change made earlier to this codebase:

```
from elasticsearch import Elasticsearch, RequestsHttpConnection, serializer, compat, exceptions

class JSONSerializerPython2(serializer.JSONSerializer):
    """Override elasticsearch library serializer to ensure it encodes utf characters during json dump.
    See original at: https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/blob/master/elasticsearch/serializer.py#L42
    A description of how ensure_ascii encodes unicode characters to ensure they can be sent across the wire
    as ascii can be found here: https://docs.python.org/2/library/json.html#basic-usage
    """
    def dumps(self, data):
        # don't serialize strings
        if isinstance(data, compat.string_types):
            return data
        try:
            return json.dumps(data, default=self.default, ensure_ascii=True)
        except (ValueError, TypeError) as e:
            raise exceptions.SerializationError(data, e)
```

I hope this helps anyone else that runs into this issue.

This is a feature request. Most file browsers nowadays have bookmarks that allow user to directly jump to a directory without going through the tree. (at least in Nautilus / Windows Explorer)

Such feature can be quite useful when a user works on many projects in various locations of the file system, because a bookmark will allow the user to quickly jump to the project and set up a 'contextual' workspace, without clicking through several layers of directory hierarchy.

Bookmarks will also allow the user to jump to a directory that's off the 'root' of the notebook server. This is useful in some setups of jupyterhub where there users also obtain a separate location for storing files in addition to their home directory. 

To minimal changes to the UX is to append bookmarks to the 'Running'  page. halted notebooks and directories can be appended to the list, probably with a gray icon to indicate they are bookmarks to inactive notebook or a folder icon to indicate it is an icon.

Bookmarks shall be broken if the original location is gone.

issue_title: can't create, commit to detached HEAD, merge
issue_body:
Various websites, and the changelog, suggest that pygit2 lets you set a detached HEAD with something like `repo.head = repo[commit_id]`. If that ever actually worked, it doesn't any more (0.22.0), because you get `"AttributeError: attribute 'head' of '_pygit2.Repository' objects is not writable"`.

(Apologies if this bug report actually reflects a problem with libgit2 rather than pygit2, but I'm unsure where the boundary between the two projects is.)

## Why this is a problem (what I’m trying to achieve)

Committing to detached HEADs is proscribed by most Git experts, but I think it's necessary in order to correctly implement editing a Git repository through a web app. When the web form that edits a particular file loads, it needs to know the id of the current commit (i.e. the commit which the version of the file being edited comes from). When the changes are made and submitted, what the web app should do is check out the commit that the edit was based on, apply the change, check out the branch again and merge it in. This way you correctly generate a merge conflict when someone comes along and makes an edit at the same time as you, and avoid clobbering edits. You effectively re-create the repository state as it was when the user started editing before doing anything.

Note that the messages `git checkout` gives when you checkout a commit, or make changes on a detached HEAD then switch back to the original branch, suggest that this is impossible, and that the only way to save commits made against a detached HEAD is to create a new named branch then merge that in. This is false: it's perfectly possible, and sensible, to use an 'anonymous branch' for this and directly merge the commit you 'left behind' into the main branch using the commit id. (And, if nothing else happened to the branch since the branch you checked out was created)

## Trying to do this with pygit2: A comedy of frustration

As mentioned above, despite it being documented that pygit2's API sensibly represents detached HEADs by returning a Commit rather than Reference object from `Repository.head`, and semi-documented that you should be able to *make* the HEAD detached by setting this property, this doesn't work.

### Other things that don't work

#### Creating a commit with an old parent directly

This would actually be my preferred solution to this sorry mess (it's actually the first thing I tried before thinking of using a detached HEAD), since it would work on a bare repo without having to make an unnecessary working tree. The error message here is `GitError: failed to create commit: current tip is not the first parent`.

#### Checking out on a bare repo

Okay, I didn't *expect* this to work, but I did kind of hope that since the whole idea of 'checking out' doesn't really make sense for a bare repo anyway, there might be some magic that made the bare repo look like a repo with a working directory for programmatic purposes. Nope. `"GitError: Cannot checkout. This operation is not allowed against bare repositories."`

Passing a `directory` argument to the checkout function makes the error go away, but then:

#### `repo.checkout_treeish(commit)`

Doing this does indeed change the working tree to reflect the commit. But it doesn't also update `repo.head` to point to the newly-checked-out commit.

This happens inside the html5lib you use. Try: `html5lib.serialize(html5lib.parseFragment(‘x‘))` Smallest "not" working example: ` import bleach tags = [ 'a', 'blockquote', 'br', 'caption', 'del', 'div', 'em', 'h1', 'h2', 'h3', 'h4', 'h5', 'h6', 'header', 'hr', 'img', 'li', 'ol', 'p', 'span', 'strong', 'sub', 'sup', 'table', 'tbody', 'td', 'tfoot', 'th', 'thead', 'tr', 'u', 'ul', ] bleach.clean("

", tags, [], [])` This will produce "

". The expected result should show the complete input string without modifying it

issue_title: Synapse cleanup: Adding 4 synapses might immediately destroy 3 of them
issue_body:
I've talked about this before, but not in a Github issue.

`Connections::createSynapse` makes room for an extra synapse, and then creates the synapse: https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/d2bc9de05be948bf699e00964c8d6c01ad64af14/src/nupic/algorithms/Connections.cpp#L126

The problem with this approach is that `createSynapse` is generally called multiple times: https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/d2bc9de05be948bf699e00964c8d6c01ad64af14/src/nupic/algorithms/TemporalMemory.cpp#L445

Depending on the initial synapse permanence, every `createSynapse` might immediately destroy the newly created synapse. So every iteration of the loop gets overwritten by the next iteration.

Instead, synapses should be cleaned up in a group, like they are in the old TP / cells4: https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/66ef718f99e860db8969646520ef2d35e3e48dc2/src/nupic/algorithms/Segment.cpp#L346

issue_title: Port existing Python unit tests to C++
issue_body: Unit tests should be included with the C++ code as part of the implementation. I'm not sure these need to match what we have in Python. It is probably better to write the tests that make the most sense based on the implementation, especially since we will end up running the Python tests against the C++ implementation as well.

issue_title: Building python bindings doesn't fail when the shared objects aren't linked properly
issue_body:
When building python bindings, even though the build may be successful, if it can't find the shared libraries for `python2.7`, `kj` and capnp` you will get an `ImportError` when trying to use the bindings from python.

Please add the following entry to [bindings/py/setup.py](https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/master/bindings/py/setup.py#L358) `commonLinkFlags`:

```
"-Wl,--no-undefined",
```

Additionally, this turned up the error that `kj` was not being found, I fixed it by adding another linker and compiler flag (this was because I did not attempt to install capnproto in a system folder, which I think is what happens when you build `nupic.core`):
```
"-L/tmp/capnproto-c++-0.5.3/build/lib"
```

issue_title: It seems that transforming PIL.Image to tensor by transforms.ToTensor is not working correctly?
issue_body:
I had problems when using transforms.ToTensor() to a float PIL Image.
And then I checked the codes, which are:
```
    # handle PIL Image
    if pic.mode == 'I':
        img = torch.from_numpy(np.array(pic, np.int32, copy=False))
    elif pic.mode == 'I;16':
        img = torch.from_numpy(np.array(pic, np.int16, copy=False))
    else:
        img = torch.ByteTensor(torch.ByteStorage.from_buffer(pic.tobytes()))
    # PIL image mode: 1, L, P, I, F, RGB, YCbCr, RGBA, CMYK
    if pic.mode == 'YCbCr':
        nchannel = 3
    elif pic.mode == 'I;16':
        nchannel = 1
    else:
        nchannel = len(pic.mode)
    img = img.view(pic.size[1], pic.size[0], nchannel)
```
In the float mode, 4 bytes are required for each pixel. Thus the buffer would return a bytes array whose size is HxWxCx4.
The code `img = img.view(pic.size[1], pic.size[0], nchannel)` should be only capable of handling the HxWxC size.

It is right?

issue_title: Annoy model on disk seems larger than expected
issue_body:
I'm trying to estimate the required memory for an annoy index on a set of vectors.  My vectors are length 128 and I've understood from other comments that annoy uses 32 bit vectors under the hook so I expect a vector to 0.5 kb.  However when I look at my annoy models on disk they seem to be ~4 times larger than that.  So an index with ~190k entries is taking about 380mb on disk rather than the 90mb I estimate just for the vector sizes.  While I can imagine some overhead over a pure mmaped numpy matrix, a factor of 4 seems quite large.  Is this reflected in memory usage as well (I haven't really tested yet)?

More generally I'd just like a way to estimate how many vectors of this size I could support with a given amount of RAM (e.g. 64 GB).

Currently the use of RNNs in tflearn is quite limited by the fact that they can only operate on fixed length sequences. This limits real world applicability quite a bit since a lot of real world data doesn't have a fixed length, or has a length that exceeds the number of steps that can be unrolled on a normal GPU. 

Also, when training a network on a continuous sequence like in the shakespeare example this makes the training a lot slower and the training set a lot bigger than it needs to be since overlapping sequences need to be fed into the network on every step, instead of just continuing from the state where the network was at before. Of course the also severely limits the attention span of the RNN.

I realize that until a short while ago this was a limitation of tensorflow itself, but they recently implemented support for dynamic RNNs and the option to iterate over variable length data. More info can be found here: https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/issues/208

I would really appreciate this feature since tflearn has been the first machine learning library I've actually been able to wrap my head around and easily get good results with.

issue_title: setup.py incorrectly detects OpenMP compilation when used with single-threaded OpenBLAS
issue_body:
OpenBLAS compiled in single-threaded mode always contains a routine `openblas_get_num_threads()`, (always returning 1 for single-threaded compilation). This means that the check for a multithreaded libcpptraj on [line 163 of `setup.py`](https://github.com/Amber-MD/pytraj/blob/master/setup.py#L163) incorrectly determines that libcpptraj is compiled with OpenMP support when libcpptraj is linked against OpenBLAS, even when serial compilation of cpptraj is requested (and performed). This currently prevents out-of-the-box Amber16 compilation when linked against OpenBLAS version 0.2.18 on Linux.

A possible fix is to change [line 163 of `setup.py`](https://github.com/Amber-MD/pytraj/blob/master/setup.py#L163) to read
```
libcpptraj_has_openmp = ([line for line in output_openmp_check 
                          if ('get_num_threads' in line.lower() 
                              and not 'openblas_get_num_threads' in line.lower()
                             )]  != [])
```

issue_title: Questions about and suggestions for build system
issue_body:
The build system currently fails with python3 as the default python interpreter (which is the case e.g. for debian jessie, gentoo, arch, ..), since the mk/* scripts use deprecated python2 syntax.

I saw comments about the python2 requirement being added and then again removed from the readme; but as far as I see it, python2 is still required.  Is this intended? What are the assumptions about the target platforms? python2 and optionally python3? If so, which versions do you want to support?

There is also at least one argument mismatch that I encountered: --help lists the "--makefiles" flag, but if you specify it, the script fails. Looking through the source reveals why: "--onlymakefiles" is the flag's name and not "--makefiles".

Now after trying "--onlymakefiles" the script still fails, this time because it's unable to find a c++ file, rendering this flag completely useless.


Is there a reason for not using e.g. python's argparse and calling it a day?

Please don't see this as criticism, I just want to understand the reasons behind the build system's design. I'm more than glad to help out with the Linux specific parts, if you need any help -- but for this I need a more precise specification of what you actually want :)

issue_title: There should be an exception in a case of "too many redirects"
issue_body:
## Long story short

When loading `http://relax-tip.de/` it redirects to `http://deepskin.de/`, but `http://deepskin.de/` redirects back to `http://relax-tip.de/`.

aiohttp has built-in limit `max_redirects=10`, but when this limit is reached users get last redirect response and no way to figure-out that is cycle redirect, as well aiohttp follows redirects automatically and it is no expected to get `304` response.

## Expected behaviour

I have an idea, when redirects limit is reached raise `TooManyRedirects` (requests do it)

## Actual behaviour

10 redirect response returned as successful response
 
## Steps to reproduce

```python
import aiohttp
import asyncio


async def main():
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        async with session.get('http://relax-tip.de/') as response:
            import ipdb; ipdb.set_trace()
            response


loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(main())
```

## Your environment

client

If such idea makes sense, I'll do pull request to implement this feature
@asvetlov @kxepal thoughts?
