issue_title: Understanding of compressing float32 to float16

issue_body: **Issue by [kneczaj](https://github.com/kneczaj)** _Tuesday May 27, 2014 at 14:30 GMT_ _Originally opened as https://github.com/jirikuncar/invenio/issues/277_ ---- I think it is sometimes helpful to be able to load single modules or classes with fixtures to Invenio database. I am going to implement this functionality. There is already `load_fixtures` function in `invenio/base/scripts/database.py`. I am going to make this function accessible from the command line. The proposed argument list is: ```python def load_fixtures(packages=[], fixture_classes=[], truncate_tables_first=False) ``` with no default packages. The function would take all the fixtures form 'fixtures' directory from packages list, and additionally add single fixtures classes from fixture_classes list. The options from command line perspective would look like below: ```python option_packages = manager.option('-p', '--packages', dest='packages', action='append', default=[], help='package import name (repeteable)') option_fixtures = manager.option('-f', '--fixture_classes', dest='fixture_classes', action='append', default=[], help='fixture classes paths to load') ``` There is also `inveniomanage populate` option, maybe it makes more sense to modify this command?

We started to rip out certain modules from Invenio core to improve maintainability and to speed up development. This issue contains some of the problems, that I faced during that process:

## Autodiscovery
**Problem:** Right now, modules have to be specified using the `PACKAGES` config variable. That makes creating overlays more difficult and error-prone, because devs need to specify the desired set twice (once in `setup.py`, a second time in `config.py`).

**Solution:** Implement entry points.

**Status:** Planned. 

## Test Overlays
**Problem:** To test a certain module, other modules that use that functionality are needed. This is especially true for visual test and automated browser tests, but also holds for presenting a good demo use case for a module.

**Solution:** Add a fake package to the modules to build a demo overlay.

**Status:** Implemented in https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-jsonschemas/pull/1 and https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/pull/3380.

##  "Module" vs "Package"
**Problem:** We somehow mix up the terms `module` and `package`. Our [cookiecutter template](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/cookiecutter-invenio-module) clearly calls the ripped out *things* modules and the Python parts packages. The dynamic loading system in `config.py` calls the single parts packages. In theory, a module can implement multiple packages, but I've never seen this in the wild and I think we do not really encourage people to do so.

**Solution:** Write some docs to clarify the module system, including the installation and loading as well as the relations `overlay->modules->packages`.

**Status:** TODO.

## require.js
**Problem:** Require.js uses [`settings.js`](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/blob/master/invenio/base/static/js/settings.js) and [`build.js`](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/blob/master/invenio/base/static/js/build.js) for getting the relations between the our JS files and vendors files correct. We've already seen that this [is not working well for overlays](https://github.com/zenodo/zenodo/blob/master/zenodo/base/static/js/settings.js#L164).

**Solution:** Replace the static version of the mentioned files with a dynamic version and some kind of registry.

**Status:** TODO.

## Upgrade Recipes
**Problem:** Right now, upgrades have to mention dependencies explicitly. Now imagine the scenario that we've faced during the [InnoDB upgrade](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/pull/3355). There we needed to fix module bugs before doing another global upgrade. Stating the fact that the core should not know about available modules, this upgrade would never be possible and [already let to some issues](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/issues/3406).

**Solution:** Implement the ability to specify a `before` relation of upgrades as well (in addition to the `after` relation).

**Status:** TODO.

## Load Order
**Problem:** Module devs currently have no way to control the load order of there module and having [cyclic dependencies](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/issues/3120#issuecomment-103816619) does not make this easier.

**Solution:** Using *Lazy{String,Dict,...}` and hope that it works? Leaving it to the overlay/site admin (i.e. `config.py`)?

**Status:** TODO.

----------------------------

**to be continued**

It seems advisable that `find_records_from_extoaiid()` also considers `CFG_OAI_ID_FIELD` for record matching.

Usecase: 

1. join2 repositories harvest each other to get copies from records. We use this process e.g. for authority records.
2. join2 repositories act as institutional bibliographies, this implies to collect all publications from a given institution (even if it's also catalogued at another partner)

In case of *authorities* the authority ID in 035 ensures deduping. Recent upcoming use cases, however, also involve harvesting of *bibliographic* records, e.g. in case two institutions from the join2 collaboration work together, we want to ensure, that our scientists have to submit their publications only once. E.g. a scientist from Jülich should only need to submit to JuSER even if he would have to submit to iMPULSE as well. Harvesting ensures that publications from JuSER are exposed to iMPULSE. It doesn't make to much sense to move the OAI-ID to another field in this case. as we're really producing a second instance of the record in another service. At the same time from a bibliographic point of view usage of 035__a for oai-ids will not work in our environment as we need a field for *the id*(tm) (ie. the one that get's stored with the linked records) in case of authority records. This is currently done via 035__.

An alternative, more complex approach might be to allow a list of marc tags for `CFG_BIBUPLOAD_EXTERNAL_OAIID_TAG` probably consolidating with `CFG_BIBUPLOAD_EXTERNAL_SYSNO_TAG`.

join2 might come up with an implementation of the simple case. (`CFG_OAI_ID_FIELD`).

issue_title: errors: exception InvalidOperationError not correctly handled by Flask
issue_body:
**Problem**

Because of this [change](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-files-rest/commit/023762ea621ae9e3dfe549d3b9fc0823d0e709c2#diff-dacc794350f23b3b0e02fd572a407601R32) now all files exceptions are subclasses of RESTException.

 `RESTException` [are managed](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-rest/blob/6f08ee8949745ca90dfc6637e09a3ac1e76c63c3/invenio_rest/views.py#L39) in a different way from invenio-rest `create_api_errorhandler()`.

Because of `InvalidOperationError` doesn't have a specific `code` set on the class, it inherits `500` from `FilesException`.
It means that is raised the 500 error instead of the 403 [set here](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-deposit/blob/master/invenio_deposit/views/rest.py#L67).

**Solutions**

Which solution make more sense in this case?

1. Set a default code `403` for the exception [InvalidOperationError](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-files-rest/blob/master/invenio_files_rest/errors.py#L49)

2. Modify the [create_api_errorhandler()](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-rest/blob/6f08ee8949745ca90dfc6637e09a3ac1e76c63c3/invenio_rest/views.py#L39) to force the status code if `'status' in kwargs`? (as done for `HTTPException`)

--------------------------------------------
ping @lnielsen @jirikuncar

As numcodecs includes source that needs to be compiled, there can be some challenges or technical hurtles that can be encountered by a user that they may not be aware of. While we do solve this in some sense by supplying `conda-forge` packages with prebuilt binaries, `pip` remains the defacto way for Python users to get packages. However in the case of `pip` the user will get the sdist, which needs compilation. While this does work, there is definitely some appeal to providing a `pip` solution that does not require compilation (i.e. prebuilt wheels). This would help users avoid compatibility problems like this one ( https://github.com/zarr-developers/numcodecs/issues/69 ).

One solution would be to piggy back off of whatever conda-forge ends up doing to also supply wheels ( https://github.com/conda-forge/conda-smithy/issues/608 ). This would work well for Windows. Though on macOS conda-forge uses the 10.9 SDK (instead of the 10.6 SDK that Python tries to support). Also on Linux conda-forge uses CentOS 6 with glibc 2.12 (instead of CentOS 5 with glibc 2.5 used by manylinux1). In practice these two cases that don't sync up will be hard for Python to require much longer and some packages already don't comply (happy to go into details if this is of interest). This certainly would enjoy the nice benefit of the architecture and community conda-forge has in place to solve these issues. It also seems that proponents of the wheel format are interested in collaborating, which definitely should help.

If that doesn't work, the alternative would be to build wheels here. For Linux, there is [manylinux1 Docker image]( https://github.com/pypa/manylinux ), which could be used fairly easily to build these. For macOS, we could try to build them here or reach out to [MacPython]( https://github.com/MacPython ) for help. For Windows, it would probably be best to reuse the conda-forge solution as much as possible as that already fits the requirements well. Probably would require some like issue ( https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/2490 ) to solved. Though I don't think anyone that has had time to do that. Alternatively one can [build a wheel with conda-build]( https://conda.io/docs/user-guide/tasks/build-packages/define-metadata.html?highlight=rpm#output-type ).

I ran into a few issues when using the library.

As a dpp user, I want to add a new column to my existing data with the result of various operations (like sum, avg, min, max, multiply, constant, join, format) performed on values of pre-defined columns of the same row, so that after running `dpp run xxx` output data has new column with value calculated for me.

## Acceptance Criteria

* [ ] able to add new column to data
* [ ] able to perform operations like sum, avg, min, max, multiply, constant, join, format

## Tasks

* [ ] prepare specs
* [ ] wtire tests
* [ ] write processor `add_fields`
* [ ] update README

## Analysis 

Spec: 

```
run: add_fields
parameters:
  resources: resource_name
  fields:
    - operation: sum
      target: new_column_name
      nullas: 0 (default)
      type: any (default)
      columns
        - column1
        - column2
    - operation: sum
      target: another_column_name
      columns:
        - column3
        - column1
    - operation: sum
      target: total_sum
      columns:
        - new_column_name
        - another_column_name
    - operation: avg/min/max/multiply/join: same as for sum
    - operation: format
      target: new_column_name
      formated-string: '{column1} is better than {column2}'
```

Available operations:

* constant - add a constant value
* sum - summed value for given column(s) in a row. None -> 0
* avg - average value from given column(s) in a row. None -> 0
* min - min value among given column(s) in a row. None -> 0
* max - max value among given column(s) in a row. None -> 0
* multiply - product of given columns(s) in a row None -> 0
* join - joins two or more column values in a row (separated by comma)
* format - allows to pass the formatted string. Eg ‘my name is {first_name}’

issue_title: 500 Points without completing the complete video. The system is calculating the duration for which the video is watched.
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

As the learner begins watching the video, it is calculating the total duration for which the video is being watched and accordingly the points are given.
For eg. A video of 2.30 min is being watched, if the learner watches till 1.30 and then closes and again starts the video and watches for 1min or more,  the learner gets 500 points and technically the video is not being completed.

### Expected behavior

The student must get 500 points only when the complete video is watched in one go.

### User-facing consequences

The admin/coach would think the student has watched the video.

issue_title: Inspect `attribute` and/or `load_from`/`dump_to` properties for marshmallow extension
issue_body:
If I create the following schema:

```python
class User(Schema):
    first_name = fields.String(attribute='firstName')
    last_name = fields.String(attribute='lastName')
```

and register it
```python
spec = APISpec(
    title='Users',
    version='1.0.0',
    plugins=[
        'apispec.ext.marshmallow',
    ],
)
spec.definition('User', schema=User)
```

the generated APISpec still lists the the properties as `first_name` and `last_name` instead of `firstName` and `lastName`. Similarly, the `load_from` and `dump_to` parameters are also ignored. 

Would there be interest/would it make sense to change the properties in the generated APISpec to reflect the `attribute`, `load_from`, and/or `dump_to` marshmallow field parameters?
As a follow-up to https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/pull/346, we may make `skip_on_field_errors` default to True.

Is possible remove None fields from serialize dumps?

Ex.:

`schema.dump({'id': 1,'value_null': None,}).data`

Want output like

`{'id': 1} `

issue_title: ModelSchema default behaviour needs a lot of overriding
issue_body:
The current behaviour of ModelSchema is not that useful to me. Where I have, say, an "order" many-to-one relationship, by default it dumps a field "order" with the order_id, and this serialised data does not then load. It would be more helpful to dump this as field "order_id". one-to-many relationships are similarly not that useful. 

Right now I am using a lot of excludes and manually defined fields, but this is unnecessary. In fact, marshmallow-sqlalchemy already has much more helpful default logic, on TableSchema, especially with include_fk=True. I can't directly use TableSchema as it only supports dump and I also need to load.

However, a one line change to schema.py really helps:

```
return converter.fields_for_model(opts.model, fields=opts.fields, exclude=opts.exclude)
-->
return converter.fields_for_table(opts.model.__table__, include_fk=True, fields=opts.fields, exclude=opts.exclude)
```

I realise this would break compatibility, but it seems a much more helpful default to me.

And I would like to use the Handling Unknown Fields functionnality:
'Turn off automatic skipping of unknown fields' [marshmallow#127](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/127)
https://marshmallow.readthedocs.io/en/3.0/quickstart.html#handling-unknown-fields

But I'm not able to import : 
`from marshmallow import INCLUDE`
=> `ImportError:` cannot import name 'INCLUDE'`

Do you when it will be integrated to Flask-marshmallow ?

When installing kinto with ``pip install kinto``, I'd like to be able to run:

    cliquet --ini config/kinto.ini serve

For now I've got to do:

    pserve config/kinto.ini

> \_\_hash\_\_() should return an integer. The only required property is that objects which compare equal have the same hash value

issue_title: replace generic exceptions with galsim exceptions
issue_body:
There are many errors that should be recoverable but galsim throws a generic
error.  This is a problem because it may not be clear from where this error
originates.  One cannot distinguish the recoverable from non-recoverable
errors.

For example, a pipeline may run many calls to galsim methods; tens is not
unusual. Each of these may throw a `RuntimeError` or `ValueError`.   The
pipeline, and galsim itself, will intersperse other python function calls with
galsim calls, many of which may throw the same errors.

The difference is that most `RuntimeError` or `ValueError` thrown by the python
standard library are not recoverable, they signify a bug in the program, and
the program should crash and the user should fix the code.

But many times when galsim throws such an error it is not a bug, galsim simply
does not know what to do with the inputs or has some internal numerical
restrictions that are not met.  Often one can just re-call the method with
modified parameters, or otherwise deal with this an move on.  If I _knew_ the
exception was a galsim exception I could write some robust code to deal with
the situation.

An example came up today when the fft code threw a `RuntimeError` because the
sheared image would require too large an FFT.  This is not a bug, I can deal
with this quite cleanly at run time, but I can't tell if this `RuntimeError`
is coming from galsim or not.

I propose replacing some uses of `RuntimeError` or `ValueError` with something
like `GalsimValueError`, `GalsimRangeError` or whatever, inheriting from a base
`GalsimError` class.    These can then be caught and dealt with robustly.

issue_title: Add hook for running surface-layer operations on photon array
issue_body:
At the hack week, @kadrlica has implemented code to sample a random selection of wavelengths from an SED (or more typically an SED * bandpass) cf. #822.  @sethdigel is working on giving the photons appropriate dxdz, dydz values on #823.  

The next step to be able to use these is to add a hook for running through a list of surface-layer operations in drawImage.  Here is the mock up that I presented in my [talk](https://project.lsst.org/meetings/lsst2016/sites/lsst.org.meetings.lsst2016/files/Sensors.pdf) about this (see p. 6 and 11):
```
prof.drawImage(stamp, method='phot', rng=rng,
               sky=sky, # need to have this now
               surface_ops=[fratio, sed, fringe],
                           # all surface-layer operations in order
               sensor=quick_ccd # several options here
 )
```
The `fratio` surface_op is what I called the thing that Seth is working on to give appropriate angles based on the f/ratio.  `sed` (which wasn't in the original presentation, but which I added here) represents code to apply Alex's wavelength sampling.  `fringe` would be something that implements @dkirkby's code for simulating fringing.  

There are more options than just these that we can consider adding down the line as well, but the point of this issue is to implement the code in `drawImage` that can run through such a list of surface operations.  i.e. something like this:
```
for op in surface_ops:
    photon_array = op.applyTo(photon_array)
```

I've noticed that files are being read in binary mode and StringIO is being used:

- [file_obj](https://github.com/django-extensions/django-extensions/blob/master/django_extensions/management/commands/sync_s3.py#L372)
- [gzipfile](https://github.com/django-extensions/django-extensions/blob/master/django_extensions/management/commands/sync_s3.py#L292)

While in python 2.x this worked (StringIO == BytesIO for Django) in python 3.x it breaks.

Do you think an acceptable fix would be to replace that StringIO import to BytesIO? (I've alreay tried it locally with success)

issue_title: IOS-XR 5.3.1 - XML agent returns incomplete tables
issue_body:
Has anyone seen this behavior ?

XML Agent on IOS-XR 5.3.1 is returning incomplete tables for at least 2 operations: NeighborTable (get_bgp_neighbors) and LLDP neighbors (get_lldp_neighbors)

On a router with 119 BGP sessions viewable with "show bgp summary" I only retrieve 3 using the get_bgp_neighbors() from napalm. Same happenswith the list of LLDP neighbors; only 7 are retrieved in the XML output when there are 15 neighbors.

The XML response is perfectly well formed, but the list is incomplete. This is reproducible on different routers running the same software. I don't have any IOS-XR 4.3 running at the moment to test.

Probably going to open a support request with Cisco TAC.

    for assoc in cls.get(**kwargs)
  File "social_core/storage.py", line 230, in openid_association
    return OpenIdAssociation(assoc.handle, base64.decodestring(secret),
  File "python3.6/base64.py", line 561, in decodestring
    return decodebytes(s)
  File "python3.6/base64.py", line 553, in decodebytes
    return binascii.a2b_base64(s)
```


This is with social_django 2.1.0.

The setting `REVOKE_TOKENS_ON_DISCONNECT` is not documented anywhere.

This is an issue because [by default](https://github.com/python-social-auth/social-core/blob/0b04306855a3219be9578f5bf57ea51720e2546f/social_core/pipeline/disconnect.py#L20) it is `False`, but the default disconnect pipeline leads you to believe that the tokens are being revoked.

```
    # Revoke any access_token when possible.
    'social_core.pipeline.disconnect.revoke_tokens',
```

Is there a reason for this to default to False or for it being undocumented?  I'm a little unfamiliar with token revocation so that's why I didn't just submit a PR to fix the documentation.

AFAIK, the solution is to add documentation somewhere in the `Configuration` section of the docs.  Also, should the default disconnect pipeline include a stage that by default does nothing?

**High-level problem**
We currently have an incomplete view on `PEM` formats. Several functions in the modules `pycrypto_keys`, `pyca_crypto_keys`, `ecdsa_keys` and  `keys` expect `PEM` header and footer to be one of:

```
-----BEGIN PUBLIC KEY----- ... -----END PUBLIC KEY-----
-----BEGIN RSA PUBLIC KEY----- ... -----END RSA PUBLIC KEY-----
-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY----- ... -----END RSA PRIVATE KEY-----
-----BEGIN EC PRIVATE KEY----- ... -----END EC PRIVATE KEY-----
```

However, there are many values for `VALUE` in `-----BEGIN {VALUE} ... -----END {VALUE}-----`, as can be seen e.g. in [openssl's pem.h](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/include/openssl/pem.h#L27-L51).


**Breaking example**
Securesystemlib's default crypto library [`cryptography`](https://cryptography.io) has changed the PEM header for encrypted RSA private keys from `PKSC#5` to `PKSC#8` (without our noticing):

```shell
# PKCS#5 header example
-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----
Proc-Type: 4,ENCRYPTED
DEK-Info: DES-EDE3-CBC,7D3EC0DB6DFBB404 ....

# PKCS#8 header example
-----BEGIN ENCRYPTED PRIVATE KEY----- ....
```

As a consequence a newly created encrypted RSA private key is not recognized as such anymore. 

```python
from securesystemslib import keys
rsa_key = keys.generate_rsa_key()
rsa_private = rsa_key["keyval"]["private"]
private_pem = keys.create_rsa_encrypted_pem(rsa_private, "1234")
keys.is_pem_private(private_pem)
# Returns `False` (should be `True`)
```
*Note: The unit tests use a [key that was generated before the update](https://github.com/secure-systems-lab/securesystemslib/blob/master/tests/data/keystore/rsa_key) and therefor pass.*


**Solutions**
1. Update our `PEM` parsing functions to account for *all* formats
*Pro:* Quick/Easy fix
*Con:* IMHO bad documentation of PEM formats, seems like a shortcut that can easily break

2. Remove `PEM` parsing functions in securesystemslib and rely on crypto libraries 
*Pro:* Seems like a more stable approach
*Con:* We support 2 crypto libraries (cryptography and pycrypto) and 3 key types (rsa, ecdsa, ed25519). And the affected functions are currently used to decide which crypto library functions should be called. Hence, this fix would require several larger changes.

Despite all, I would prefer solution (2).


**Further readings**
Below are random sources about PEM, PKCS5 and PKCS8. I didn't find the related RFC's very helpful, but I'll probably have to dig deeper:

- [PKCS#8 on wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PKCS_8)
- [More info on PKCS#5 and 8](https://github.com/kjur/jsrsasign/wiki/Tutorial-for-PKCS5-and-PKCS8-PEM-private-key-formats-differences)
- [Interesting discussion about PKCS in `pyca`](https://github.com/pyca/pyopenssl/issues/206)
- [`pycrypto` PEM loading](https://www.dlitz.net/software/pycrypto/api/current/Crypto.PublicKey.RSA.RSAImplementation-class.html#importKey)
- [`cryptography` PEM loading](https://cryptography.io/en/latest/hazmat/primitives/asymmetric/rsa/#key-loading)
- [PEM formats in openssl](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/include/openssl/pem.h#L27-L51)

issue_title: [Documentation Issue] Pub/Sub default max_messages is unclear in 0.13.0 docs
issue_body:
Page Name: pubsub-usage
Release: 0.13.0

This section: 

> Fetch pending messages for a pull subscription:

```
>>> from gcloud import pubsub
>>> client = pubsub.Client()
>>> topic = client.topic('topic_name')
>>> subscription = topic.subscription('subscription_name')
>>> with topic.batch() as batch:
...     batch.publish('this is the first message_payload')

>>> received = subscription.pull()  # API request

>>> [message.message_id for message in messages]

['this is the first message_payload', 'this is the second message_payload']
>>> [message.attributes for message in messages]

Does not pull all pending messages in my very simple test code.  It only pulls one pending message.  I can get multiple by specifying 'max_messages' greater than 1 in the 'subscription.pull()' call.


Has something changed in the implementation since this doc was written?

issue_title: Pub/Sub has no way to track errors from the subscriber thread.
issue_body:
Presently if you subscribe and an error occurs in the consumer's thread there's no way to respond to the error on the main thread.

It just logs the exception and hangs:

```python
Exception in thread Consumer helper: consume bidirectional stream:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/jonwayne/.pyenv/versions/3.6.0/lib/python3.6/threading.py", line 916, in _bootstrap_inner
    self.run()
  File "/Users/jonwayne/.pyenv/versions/3.6.0/lib/python3.6/threading.py", line 864, in run
    self._target(*self._args, **self._kwargs)
  File "/Users/jonwayne/workspace/python-docs-samples/pubsub/cloud-client/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/google/cloud/pubsub_v1/subscriber/_consumer.py", line 248, in _blocking_consume
    self._policy.on_exception(exc)
  File "/Users/jonwayne/workspace/python-docs-samples/pubsub/cloud-client/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/google/cloud/pubsub_v1/subscriber/policy/thread.py", line 135, in on_exception
    raise exception
  File "/Users/jonwayne/workspace/python-docs-samples/pubsub/cloud-client/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/google/cloud/pubsub_v1/subscriber/_consumer.py", line 234, in _blocking_consume
    for response in response_generator:
  File "/Users/jonwayne/workspace/python-docs-samples/pubsub/cloud-client/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/grpc/_channel.py", line 363, in __next__
    return self._next()
  File "/Users/jonwayne/workspace/python-docs-samples/pubsub/cloud-client/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/grpc/_channel.py", line 357, in _next
    raise self
grpc._channel._Rendezvous: <_Rendezvous of RPC that terminated with (StatusCode.NOT_FOUND, Resource not found (resource=my-subs).)>
```

It seems that `consumer.open` should return a future. This future could be used to both block the main thread to consume message and pass through exceptions.

So the current sample:

```python
def receive_messages(project, subscription_name):
    """Receives messages from a pull subscription."""
    subscriber = pubsub_v1.SubscriberClient()
    subscription_path = subscriber.subscription_path(
        project, subscription_name)

    def callback(message):
        print('Received message: {}'.format(message))
        message.ack()

    subscriber.subscribe(subscription_path, callback=callback)

    # The subscriber is non-blocking, so we must keep the main thread from
    # exiting to allow it to process messages in the background.
    print('Listening for messages on {}'.format(subscription_path))
    while True:
        time.sleep(60)
```

Could instead be:

```python
def receive_messages(project, subscription_name):
    """Receives messages from a pull subscription."""
    subscriber = pubsub_v1.SubscriberClient()
    subscription_path = subscriber.subscription_path(
        project, subscription_name)

    def callback(message):
        print('Received message: {}'.format(message))
        message.ack()

    future = subscriber.subscribe(subscription_path, callback=callback)

    print('Listening for messages on {}'.format(subscription_path))
    # The subscriber is non-blocking but returns a future that allows us
    # to block the main thread and allow messages to process in the
    # background. This effectively blocks forever unless an error
    # occurs in the subscriber thread in which case it'll be
    # re-raised here.
    future.result()
```

There does not seem to be any documentation for what retry mechanisms are implemented by the clients.  Would be wonderful if this were highlighted, explicitly.  These cases are important in particular for users to decide whether to implement their own error handling:
1.  Re-tryable errors.
2. Non-retryable errors.
3. Prolonged non-retry-able errors (hours).

issue_title: Can't download encrypted files larger than 1 MB
issue_body:
I'm using `upload_from_file` with `encryption_key` to store files inside a Google Cloud Storage bucket. This works very well.

Now I'm trying to download these files again. Using `download_to_file` with the same `encryption_key`. This crashes with the following stack trace:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "restore.py", line 40, in <module>
    main()
  File "restore.py", line 37, in main
    download_from_storage(args.file, args.bucket, args.credentials, key)
  File "restore.py", line 17, in download_from_storage
    blob.download_to_file(my_file, encryption_key=encryption_key)
  File "/Users/daniel/Projects/python/gcs-backup/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/google/cloud/storage/blob.py", line 354, in download_to_file
    download.initialize_download(request, client._connection.http)
  File "/Users/daniel/Projects/python/gcs-backup/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/google/cloud/streaming/transfer.py", line 390, in initialize_download
    self.stream_file(use_chunks=True)
  File "/Users/daniel/Projects/python/gcs-backup/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/google/cloud/streaming/transfer.py", line 614, in stream_file
    response = self._process_response(response)
  File "/Users/daniel/Projects/python/gcs-backup/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/google/cloud/streaming/transfer.py", line 528, in _process_response
    raise TransferRetryError(response.content)
google.cloud.streaming.exceptions.TransferRetryError: The target object is encrypted by a customer-supplied encryption key.
```

I'm using `google-cloud-storage==0.20.0`.

This only occurs when downloading files larger than 1mb. Any file less than 1mb is downloaded and decrypted as expected. A file larger than 1mb might be downloaded in chunks and only the first 1mb chunk is saved to disk.

**Steps to reproduce**
1) Upload a file larger than 1mb using a custom encryption key
2) Try to download this file using the same encryption key
3) Find a 1mb chunk of your file at the destination

**demo code**
```
#!/usr/bin/env python

from tempfile import NamedTemporaryFile
import os

from google.cloud import storage
from google.cloud.storage import Blob

CREDENTIALS = './credentials.json'
BUCKET_NAME = '< SET BUCKET >'
ENCRYPTION_KEY = 'v3CtoFyEJmj52RGsSqze7C8fD6mzgpnd'
FILE_NAME = 'enc-test-file'

client = storage.Client.from_service_account_json(CREDENTIALS)
bucket = client.get_bucket(BUCKET_NAME)


def upload_to_storage(filename):
    blob = Blob(FILE_NAME, bucket)

    with open(filename, 'rb') as my_file:
        blob.upload_from_file(my_file, encryption_key=ENCRYPTION_KEY)


def download_from_storage(filename):
    blob = Blob(FILE_NAME, bucket)

    with open(filename, 'wb') as my_file:
        blob.download_to_file(my_file, encryption_key=ENCRYPTION_KEY)


def main():
    size = 2097152  # 2mb file

    f = NamedTemporaryFile(delete=False)
    f.write("\0" * size)
    f.close()

    print('Uploading {} to Google Cloud Storage...'.format(f.name))
    upload_to_storage(f.name)

    print('Downloading {} from Google Cloud Storage...'.format(f.name))
    download_from_storage(f.name)

    os.remove(f.name)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

This might be because the encryption headers aren't added to the request for the next chunk.

Follow up from #2744.

Is there some kind of status or message we could add to https://pypi.python.org/pypi/gcloud to direct people to https://pypi.python.org/pypi/google-cloud?

Or possibly set the category to `Development Status :: 7 - Inactive`?
Can look at this, leaving as note as reminder.

issue_title: Running dev_appserver on remote machine behind a proxy
issue_body:
I have managed to put myself into a very special circumstance (or maybe OS vendors have), where I can't find a stable project development solution, which I would enjoy; I have been using Chromebook for development with Cloud 9 and alike services. I like the paradigm of developing on remote servers, because you don't have to deal with the bugs and system malfunctions, which tend to plague the virtualization tools on Windows and OS X (people say they work, but I think they haven't tried cloud development paradigm). I have been very happy developer for past three years... until I switched jobs and have to use HTTP development tools like the admin panel of AppEngine.

Some tools bend to my will more easily than others. With AppEngine the problem is, that you are checking the Host headers for security reasons and only allow localhost, I tried overriding that with a header rewrite, but you use relative URIs only, which read the Host header; so either I can not access datastore features or the links do not work, which makes the panel quite useless.

Some other tools, which do bend to my will more easily have solved this issue by allowing you to define root_path variable; so in the admin panel software you will use absolute paths, which can be defaulted to the Host and overridden if such variable is present.

I believe I could also just buy a domain, but some users might not want to do that.

Of course there might already be a solution, but I haven't been able to discover it, so feel free to point me towards right direction.

5. Steps to reproduce

table = dataset.table(TABLE_NAME, SCHEMA)
for row in table.fetch_data():
  make_something()

Using GitHub flavored markdown can help make your request clearer.
See: https://guides.github.com/features/mastering-markdown/

Hello,

I have found that there is a maximum on the number of entities that can be saved at once and the number of IDs that can be allocated at once.

Please document them!

https://cloud.google.com/datastore/docs/tools/administration

This is related to the issue opened here ( https://github.com/materialsproject/fireworks/issues/97 ). It seemed relevant to raise this here if `monty` plays a role in how `fireworks` performs serialization. There are few relevant points to raise here.

1. Current serialization is too slow, something like `tostring` would be much faster (but less readable).
2. Serialization could be more compact using some form of fast compression. (`blosc` would be worth considering)
3. Large arrays will not fit into a single document in MongoDB. (could use GridFS or an external file system with filenames saved in BSON)

The first point could be implemented very easily, but `tolist` remains the more readable solution. So, it would be worth making using `tostring` optional somehow. As the name suggests, this returns a string, which is acceptable in JSON

The second point is less important, but may help avoid having to deal with the third point immediately. Using `blosc` is relatively straightforward, there are already nice Python bindings for this ( https://github.com/ContinuumIO/python-blosc ). The benchmarking included already shows how simple this is to do. It is worth noting that `blosc` is very fast and is designed for frequent transmission. This returns a string, which is acceptable in JSON.

The third point may take more or less work depending on the strategy chosen. An easy way to solve this would be to use HDF5 outside of MongoDB and simply store the filenames with dataset in the MongoDB documents. HDF5 has its own ways to do compression, but third party compression strategies can be used, as well. Here `h5py` is probably the best way to go if HDF5 is selected. The alternative could be using GridFS (compression would be tricky). Being able to adjust block sizes would be nice if GridFS is selected.

issue_title: Dry run not working with transactions is slow
issue_body:
It appears that when running with transactions enabled, the semantics of dry_run changes. This means that in preview mode it will potentially start doing all kinds of crazy database stuff, only to rollback the transaction in case the user doesn't confirm the changes. For our app with a rather complicated save, this makes preview 10 times slower than it should be.

The following patch reverts so that dry_run can be used with transactions as well:

https://github.com/arj03/django-import-export/commit/528ca8fbfc3ad342b4365804a1e9e6a487b84c61

I don't see why we can't both have transactions and dry_run, they should be orthogonal.

`
_REPO_PATH = "C:\my_git_repo"

repo = git.Repo(repo_path)
repo.git.update_environment(GIT_SSH=_SSH_EXECUTABLE_PATH)
fetch_results = repo.remote('origin').fetch()
`
Output of this is

Please make sure you have the correct access rights

`
#!/usr/bin/python

import os

import subprocess
import traceback

with open(os.path.join(os.path.expanduser("~"), ".ssh", "test_file"), 'w') as outfile:

		outfile.write('Environ = %s\n' % os.environ)

		outfile.write('\n')

File `test_file` is created, when I run the script, so it is executed.

If I run `GIT_SSH=~/.ssh/ssh_executable.py git fetch -v origin` via git console I will be asked for a password and then everything will be Ok. So, I expect, that when I run my script it will ask passphrase of my key, but it just fails.
I rechecked, that my GIT_SSH_KEY environment variable contains right path to my key. Also, it has the name `my_key`, not `id_rsa`.
I'm running Windows 10.

Thanks.

issue_title: [discovery] Add ability to check doc status
issue_body: The Discovery API exposes a get doc endpoint to check the status of a document after ingestion (https://www.ibm.com/watson/developercloud/discovery/api/v1/#get-doc) but this is not available via the sdk.

After discussion on `#rdo`, `update-patches` should be preserved as an alias to `rdopkg patch -l` because penalty for forgetting `-l` is a lost patches branch so its conveniet to have a safe alias for that.

Try to reuse as much of `patch` code path as possible while preserving current `update-patches` functionality.

Remove obsolete warning, rather explain the link between the two actions.
